content.text: • One encodes ‘1’s by a change in the output signal (version ‘a’ below), and ‘0’s by no change; the other does the reverse (version ‘b’ below).
• However, like NRZ, it retains the same value for the entire symbol period.
• Version (a) is used where there can be long runs of ‘1’s – as a result of block coding.
• Version (b) is used useful where there are a large number of consecutive ‘0’s – often as a result of bit-stuffing.
